text: The robotic-assisted repair of a right inguinal hernia begins with an incision in the peritoneum, moving from lateral to midline. The hernia is visible as a hole in the abdominal wall. The surgeon dissects tissues from the peritoneal lining, which forms the hernia sac, with minimal bleeding. The lining is seen forming the sac and entering the hernia defect.

The surgeon retracts the hernia contents, including fat, back into the abdominal cavity. The pubis and Cooper's ligament are identified as anatomical landmarks for the medial point of dissection. The robotic instruments provide enhanced visualization and precise manipulation during the procedure.

The surgeon has reduced the hernia sac and its contents, placing a self-fixating mesh over the hernia defect. The mesh adheres to the abdominal wall without the need for stitches or tacks. The peritoneum is then closed over the mesh using a locking absorbable suture, preventing the mesh from adhering to the intestines and utilizing the hernia sac and fat to reinforce the closure.
